channel.title: NYT > Most Recent Headlines
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: How Solvers Keep Their Longest Wordle and Crossword Streaks
item.description: Meet the players who persist through childbirth or intense athletic feats to ensure they solve Wordle and other puzzles every day.
item.dc:creator: Elie Levine

item.title: Frustrated by Courts, Trump Weighed Suspending a Constitutional Right
item.description: Secret memos show that the White House debated last year, to a greater degree than previously known, whether to limit habeas corpus rights for undocumented immigrants.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan

media:content.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Stephen Miller, left, and Will Scharf, right, in the Oval Office earlier this year.

item.title: A Three-Step Plan to Undo Trump’s Assault on Democracy
item.description: Local elections, and local outreach, can be crucial for stopping gerrymanders.
item.dc:creator: Ben Wikler

item.title: In Alabama, Opposition to Renewable Solar Energy Joins a Data Center Battle
item.description: Tuesday’s runoff for a slot on the Alabama Public Service Commission has a familiar ring to it, with talk of data centers and electricity costs. But in a southern twist, solar power has joined the list of villains.
item.dc:creator: David W. Chen and Audra Melton

media:content.media:credit: Audra Melton for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The Alabama Public Service Commission has suddenly become a hot-button issue ahead of Tuesday’s runoff primaries.

item.title: Is There Such a Thing as Timeless Jeans?
item.description: A reader wants to know if the denim trend wagon will ever stop.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman

media:content.media:credit: Getty Images

item.title: The ‘No Kings’ Protest Killing With a Novel Legal Twist
item.description: A gunman believed he was defending the crowd. He killed an innocent bystander. Should he be convicted of a crime?
item.dc:creator: Kurt Streeter

media:content.media:credit: Kim Raff for The New York Times

item.title: How Quebec Left the Church and Became the World Leader in Assisted Dying
item.description: A society once strongly Roman Catholic has rejected the church’s prohibition on euthanasia, with many instead viewing control over one’s death as an individual right.
item.dc:creator: Norimitsu Onishi and Hubert Hayaud

media:content.media:description: In Lanaudière, 13 of 100 people end their lives through medically assisted dying.

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Nord Stream Conspiracy,’ by Bojan Pancevski
item.description: In “The Nord Stream Conspiracy,” the investigative journalist Bojan Pancevski tells a high-stakes international war story in blockbuster prose.
item.dc:creator: Robert F. Worth
item.category: The Nord Stream Conspiracy: The Inside Story of the Explosions That Shook the World (Book)

media:content.media:credit: Danish Defense/Cover Images, via Reuters
media:content.media:description: The aftermath of the Nord Stream pipeline explosion in 2022.

item.title: Why the Art Workers’ Coalition’s Battles Still Resonate
item.description: Members of the Art Workers’ Coalition fought for a fairer and more equitable business for themselves. Their battles resonate to this day.
item.dc:creator: Max Lakin

item.title: 8 Proposals for a Better Art World
item.description: Artists on what a more utopian version of their field might look like.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Russeth

item.title: A History of American Utopia in 10 Acts
item.description: Exploring some of the significant experiments that have best captured the country’s idealistic spirit.
item.dc:creator: Zoë Lescaze

media:content.media:credit: Michael Ochs Archives/Getty Images
media:content.media:description: Members of Parliament-Funkadelic pose for a portrait inside the Mothership around 1977.

item.title: American Utopia: Why Artists Can’t Stop Imagining a Better World
item.description: T’s annual Art issue explores how the idea of a good if elusive place has inspired great art, and vice versa.
item.dc:creator: M.H. Miller

item.title: An Oral History of Casa de Estrogen, L.A.’s Haven for Queer, Feminist Art
item.description: How one apartment building in L.A.’s Koreatown became a haven for queer, feminist art in the 1990s and beyond.
item.dc:creator: Cassie Packard

item.title: To Toast America’s Birthday, They Blended Bourbon From Every State
item.description: United States of Bourbon, the result of a Vermont couple’s many road trips, is just one of several special whiskeys marking the 250th.
item.dc:creator: Liza Weisstuch

media:content.media:credit: Oliver Parini for The New York Times

item.title: Millions of Women Are Left Out of Menopause’s Moment
item.description: Women who can’t take hormone therapy because of breast cancer or other diseases say they are deeply frustrated.
item.dc:creator: Liz Krieger

media:content.media:credit: Jillian Freyer for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Cybele Malone cannot take hormone replacement therapy because of her history of breast cancer.

item.title: Inside Colombia’s Never-Ending Drug War
item.description: Ten years after a landmark peace deal, armed groups are wreaking deadly violence, blending jungle combat with new drone warfare.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní and Federico Rios

media:content.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: FARC guerrillas in Norte de Santander, Colombia, where they are clashing with another armed group, the ELN.

item.title: Is This the End of Political Islam?
item.description: For decades, Islamic governance held allure in the Middle East. Now some scholars say the Islamist wave has passed.
item.dc:creator: Ben Hubbard
item.category: Middle East and North Africa Unrest (2010- )

media:content.media:credit: Nanna Heitmann for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: A damaged photograph of Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini at the headquarters of Iran’s state TV broadcaster in Tehran after an Israeli strike last year.

item.title: Are Glutathione Supplements a Scam?
item.description: The so-called “mother of all antioxidants” is sold as dissolving strips, capsules, liquids, gels and powders. We asked experts if they actually work.
item.dc:creator: Katie Wright

item.title: One City Might Have Just Cracked the Housing Crisis
item.description: A large real estate development in the middle of Vancouver, British Columbia, shows how cities can build more housing.
item.dc:creator: Binyamin Appelbaum

item.title: Uzbekistan Is Attracting Younger Travelers, but Does It Live Up to the Buzz?
item.description: This Central Asian country is gaining traction with young travelers seeking beauty, authenticity and low prices. Was it worth the 12-hour flight?
item.dc:creator: Gabe Castro-Root

item.title: Taking on Neo-Nazis, One Trademark at a Time
item.description: In Germany, a campaign called Rights Against the Right is securing trademarks for right-wing symbols and phrases, aiming to cut into a revenue stream that sustains hate groups.
item.dc:creator: Aishvarya Kavi

media:content.media:credit: Schore Mehrdju for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Among the latest trademarks awarded to Rights Against was for the term “ess ess,” code for S.S., or Schutzstaffel, the elite guard of the Nazi Reich.

item.title: Una papa misteriosa en el cielo de Colorado aparece en los archivos sobre ovnis
item.description: Las papas ya han aparecido en avistamientos anteriores, así como en viajes espaciales reales y de ficción.
item.dc:creator: Danny Hakim
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Iranian Team Arrives in U.S. for World Cup After Months of Uncertainty
item.description: The war had stirred doubts about the team’s participation in the tournament. It is set to play New Zealand in a Los Angeles area stadium on Monday.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:credit: Andre Penner/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Iran’s players arriving at a stadium near Los Angeles on Sunday.

item.title: Trump Hosts U.F.C. Event at the White House
item.description: President Trump turned 80 on Sunday and celebrated with a $60 million Ultimate Fighting Championship event at the White House.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Paik

media:content.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times

item.title: U.S. and Iran Reach Framework for Peace
item.description: The United States and Iran reached a framework for peace on Sunday. Trump said the agreement would reopen the Strait of Hormuz after a monthslong blockade.
item.dc:creator: Jiawei Wang
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Tehran last week.

item.title: Son of Norway’s Crown Princess Is Convicted of Rape
item.description: Marius Borg Hoiby was sentenced to four years in prison. He stood trial as his mother came under pressure over her ties to Jeffrey Epstein.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel and Henrik Pryser Libell

media:content.media:credit: Lise Aserud/NTB Scanpix, via Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Marius Borg Hoiby of Norway, left, in Oslo in 2022.

item.title: A 80 años de la Segunda Guerra Mundial, Alemania y Japón se rearman
item.description: Berlín y Tokio fueron aliados en la década de 1940, con resultados desastrosos. Ahora vuelven a unir fuerzas.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley and Javier C. Hernández
item.category: Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
United States Defense and Military Forces

media:content.media:credit: Foto de consorcio por David Mareuil
media:content.media:description: Shinjiro Koizumi, ministro de Defensa de Japón, y su homólogo alemán, Boris Pistorius, en una base naval en Yokosuka, Japón, en marzo. Ambos países han estado reforzando sus ejércitos.

item.title: Britain Announces Social Media Ban for Children
item.description: Prime Minister Keir Starmer said his government would bar children under 16 from social media starting in 2027. The move follows similar policies in Australia and several other countries.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia

media:content.media:credit: Katie Collins/Reuters
media:content.media:description: High school students in London earlier this year.

item.title: She Didn’t Live to See the Knicks Championship. She Would Have Loved It.
item.description: It’s finally the Knicks’ time. But the five-decade title drought, full of both joy and pain, was all some devoted fans were ever able to experience.
item.dc:creator: Harvey Araton

media:content.media:credit: Geoff Burke/Imagn Images, via Reuters
media:content.media:description: Jalen Brunson, the Knicks’ All-Star point guard, gave long-suffering fans realistic dreams of winning a title when he joined the team in 2022.

item.title: On the Horizon: A Maritime Spectacle 10 Times the Size of Fleet Week
item.description: New York’s two-day 250th extravaganza will feature the country’s largest parade of tall ships and warships, but adds daunting logistical challenges to an already overwrought summer.
item.dc:creator: Dana Rubinstein and John Ismay

media:content.media:credit: Olga Ginzburg for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The Tuiga is one of roughly 90 vessels taking part in Sail4th 250 on July 3 and 4.

item.title: NYT Spelling Bee Answers for June 15, 2026
item.description: Feeling stuck on today’s puzzle? We can help.
item.dc:creator: New York Times Games

item.title: If Democrats Want to Win the House, Their Battle May Start Here
item.description: A bitterly fought Democratic primary in New York’s Hudson Valley will determine who takes on Representative Mike Lawler, one of the more vulnerable Republicans this cycle.
item.dc:creator: Grace Ashford

media:content.media:credit: Ryan Murphy for The New York Times

item.title: Plaintiffs in Catholic Church Abuse Case Weigh $800 Million Offer
item.description: The proposed settlement would pay $250,000 to each of the 1,300 people who have sued the Archdiocese of New York over sexual abuse claims. But for any of them to get the deal, all must agree to take it.
item.dc:creator: Maya King and Jeffery C. Mays

media:content.media:credit: Anna Watts for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Anthony Santucci said he was abused as a teenager at a Catholic church in the Bronx.

item.title: Iran War Live Updates: U.S. and Tehran Agree on Framework for Peace
item.description: The deal was expected to open the Strait of Hormuz, lift the U.S. naval blockade on Iranian ports and pave the way for further talks. It did not address Iran’s nuclear program.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

media:content.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times

item.title: 5 minutos para que te enamores de Nina Simone
item.description: En una frase era capaz de pasar del susurro al rugido y su repertorio abarca estándares de jazz, canciones de protesta, blues y pop. Escucha estas 11 canciones favoritas
item.dc:creator: Marcus J. Moore

item.title: Word of the Day: apocalyptic
item.description: This word has appeared in 227 articles on NYTimes.com in the past year. Can you use it in a sentence?
item.dc:creator: The Learning Network

item.title: Historic Orthodox Cathedral Burns in Russian Attack on Kyiv
item.description: A bishop reported that many holy items had been recovered from the cathedral, at the site of the Perchersk monastery, a revered place for Ukrainian and Russian Orthodox faiths.
item.dc:creator: Maria Varenikova

media:content.media:credit: Evgeniy Maloletka/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Rescue workers fighting a fire at the Dormition Cathedral after a Russian attack in Kyiv on Monday.

item.title: Many Iranians Express Relief Over Agreement to End the War
item.description: After enduring months of conflict, ordinary people in Iran were relieved to hear about the deal. Opposition groups were disappointed.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi

media:content.media:credit: Vahid Salemi/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Demonstrators in Tehran on Sunday.

item.title: Las Culturistas Culture Awards and 6 More Things to Watch on TV This Week
item.description: Bowen Yang and Matt Rogers host their pop culture award show, and “Summer House” airs a surprise episode.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez

media:content.media:credit: Monty Brinton/Bravo

item.title: Todd Blanche Is a Dangerous Choice for Trump’s Attorney General
item.description: Nominating Mr. Blanche to be the nation’s top law enforcement officer crosses a red line, and the Senate needs to defeat him.
item.dc:creator: The Editorial Board

media:content.media:credit: Ethan Noah Roy

item.title: No Corrections: June 15, 2026
item.description: No corrections appeared in print on Monday, June 15, 2026.

item.title: Quote of the Day: Pinch Yourselves, Knicks Fans: Next Year Is Now
item.description: Quotation of the Day for Monday, June 15, 2026.

item.title: Hormuz Reopening Would Offer Relief for Asia, but Economic Scars Will Remain
item.description: For months, Asia has suffered a physical supply crunch that will likely drag on its economies long after the crucial waterway reopens.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:credit: Reuters
media:content.media:description: Ships in the Strait of Hormuz in Oman last week. More than four-fifths of the petroleum and liquefied natural gas transiting the strait is typically bound for Asian markets.

item.title: U.S. and Iran Reach Agreement to Reopen Strait and Begin Nuclear Talks
item.description: The accord halts hostilities in the war that began in February but leaves unresolved the critical issue of Iran’s nuclear program.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Swan, Farnaz Fassihi and Ronen Bergman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament

media:content.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Gathering in Tehran earlier this month., Iran affirmed in the agreement that it would never seek to build or procure a nuclear weapon, but the regime has made that promise many times previously.

item.title: U.F.C. Viewing Party Draws Thousands to White House Ellipse
item.description: People from all over the country came to Washington on Sunday for a unique viewing party of the Ultimate Fighting Championship bouts being held on the South Lawn of the White House.
item.dc:creator: Clarence Williams

media:content.media:credit: Alex Kent
media:content.media:description: U.F.C. fans gather at the Ellipse to watch the U.F.C. Freedom 250 outside of the White House on Sunday.

item.title: Singer Oliver Tree Is Said to Have Died in Collision of Helicopters in Brazil
item.description: The two helicopters crashed in Rio de Janeiro on Sunday, killing at least six people, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri

media:content.media:credit: Tercio Teixeira/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
media:content.media:description: The helicopters crashed over a car dealership in Rio de Janeiro on Sunday.

item.title: Trump pierde terreno entre la clase trabajadora blanca
item.description: Un análisis de los datos de las encuestas muestra un cambio extraordinario entre los votantes blancos de clase trabajadora respecto a la gestión económica del presidente.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher

media:content.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Entre los votantes blancos de clase trabajadora, la valoración del presidente Trump en relación con el costo de la vida se situó en solo un 36 por ciento en una encuesta de The New York Times.

item.title: Trump’s U.F.C. Event Comes as Backing of Young Men, Once a Strength, Wanes
item.description: Some Republicans saw a political opening in Sunday’s fights at the White House. Democrats said they saw a distraction from more pressing matters.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner

media:content.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Police on horseback outside of the U.F.C. event at the White House on Sunday.

item.title: California Sues to Stop One County’s Ban on Most Mail Voting
item.description: Shasta County voters had approved a ballot measure that would have heavily restricted voting by mail and imposed photo ID requirements. State officials said the new rules were illegal.
item.dc:creator: Maia Spoto

media:content.media:credit: Mike Kai Chen for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Ballot processing in Shasta County in 2024.

item.title: U.S. and Iran Reach Framework for Peace
item.description: The deal was expected to open the Strait of Hormuz and lift the U.S. naval blockade on Iranian ports. But it would leave the thorniest nuclear issues for another day.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

media:content.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
